1: haggᵉdûd (H1416) — the raiding party, band; the Arabic ʿărāḇîm as nomadic raiders who killed all the older sons in the camp. The phrase haggᵉdûd habbāʾ ḇammaḥăneh is the raiding contingent in the army camp. The Chronicler explains why Ahaziah — the youngest — becomes king: not dynastic choice but survival necessity. The parallel in 2 Kgs 9:27-29 gives the death in a slightly different sequence.
2: ben-ʾarbaʿîm ûšetayim šānāh ʾAḥazyāhû (‘Ahaziah was forty-two years old’) — a well-known textual difficulty: 2 Kgs 8:26 reads ben-ʿeśrîm ûštayim (‘twenty-two years old’). Since his father Jehoram died at forty (21:5, 20), an age of forty-two for Ahaziah is impossible. The MT of Chronicles has an error (ʾarbaʿîm for ʿeśrîm) probably introduced by scribal confusion between the Hebrew numerals. The LXX Codex Alexandrinus reads twenty-two. The MKT follows the MT but readers should note the reading in 2 Kgs 8:26. bat-ʿOmrî (‘daughter of Omri’) — Athaliah is the daughter of Ahab (so explicitly in v2 of the same verse), but Omri is named as the dynasty-founder; the identification by dynastic grandfather is a formal title.
3: gam-hûʾ hālaḵ bᵉdarᵉḵê bêt ʾAḥʾāḇ (‘he too walked in the ways of the house of Ahab’) — the adverb gam (‘also, too’) links Ahaziah to his father Jehoram (21:6). yôʿaṣtô lᵉrāšaʿ — from yaʿaṣ (H3289, advise) and rāšaʿ (H7561, act wickedly): she counseled him to wickedness. The mother as evil advisor is a rare explicit characterization; the Chronicler attributes Ahaziah’s failure to deliberate domestic influence rather than autonomous decision.
4: wayyaʿaś hāraʿ bᵉʿênê YHWH kᵉbêt ʾAḥʾāḇ — the standard condemnation formula applied to the Ahab dynasty. hēm-hāyû lô yôʿᵃṣîm ʾaḥᵃrê môt ʾāḇîw lᵉmašḥît lô (‘after his father’s death they became his advisers and led him to his ruin’) — mašḥît (H4889): destruction, ruin; the Hiphil participle of šāḥat (to destroy). The Ahab court functionaries outlived Jehoram and continued their influence into Ahaziah’s reign.
5: bᵉʿaṣātām hālaḵ (‘following their advice’) — ʿēṣāh (H6098): advice, counsel; the connection back to v3-4 closes the counsel theme. The battle at Ramoth-gilead with Hazael of Syria (Ḥăzāʾēl melek ʾĂrām) recycles the battle where Ahab died (1 Kgs 22); now his son Joram is wounded there. wayyakkû hāʾărammîm ʾet-Yôrām (‘the Syrians wounded Joram’) — nākāh (H5221): to smite, wound.
6: Rāmāh — a shortened form of Ramoth-gilead; the wounded Joram recovers in Jezreel. The Chronicler uses both the full and short forms in proximity (Ramoth-gilead in v5, Ramah here), maintaining fidelity to his sources. Ahaziah’s visit to the sick Joram — liryʾōt ʾet-Yôrām ben-ʾAḥʾāḇ bᵉYizrᵉʿēl kî-ḥôleh hûʾ — becomes the occasion for his death (vv7-9).
7: ûmēʾĕlōhîm hāyᵉtāh tᵉbûʾat ʾAḥazyāhû lābôʾ ʾel-Yôrām (‘Ahaziah’s downfall came about through God’s design’) — mēʾĕlōhîm: literally ‘from God’; the prepositional phrase marks divine causation behind the political sequence. tᵉbûʾāh (H8395) is an unusual word for ruin/downfall — used only here and Ps 89:41 in this sense. The Chronicler attributes Ahaziah’s death at Jehu’s hand to YHWH’s providential design: Jehu was mĕšûaḥ YHWH lᵉhakrît ʾet-bêt ʾAḥʾāḇ (‘anointed by the LORD to destroy the house of Ahab’).
8: ûḇᵉhiššāpᵉṭ Yēhûʾ ʿim-bêt ʾAḥʾāḇ (‘while Jehu was carrying out judgment on the house of Ahab’) — šāpaṭ (H8199): to judge, execute judgment; the Niphal hiššāpᵉṭ = to contend, execute, go to judgment with. śārê Yᵉhûdāh ûḇᵉnê ʾaḥê ʾAḥazyāhû mᵉšārᵉtîm lᵉʾAḥazyāhû (‘the princes of Judah and the sons of Ahaziah’s brothers who were in attendance on him’) — šārat (H8334): to minister, serve; these attendants were killed with Ahaziah’s delegation.
9: wayyᵉḇaqqēš ʾet-ʾAḥazyāhû (‘Jehu searched for Ahaziah’) — bāqaš (H1245): to seek, search for; found hiding in Samaria (mistattēr bᵉŠōmᵉrôn). The burial eulogy preserved: kî-ʾāmar ben-Yᵉhôšāpāṭ hûʾ ʾăšer-dāraš ʾet-YHWH bᵉḵāl-lᵉḇāḇô (‘for they said, he is the grandson of Jehoshaphat, who sought the LORD with all his heart’) — dāraš (H1875): to seek, inquire; the Chronicler’s key verb for genuine religious devotion. The grandfather’s commendation survives as the only positive note for Ahaziah’s burial.
10: watāqom ʾAtalyāhû ʾēm ʾAḥazyāhû (‘Athaliah the mother of Ahaziah rose up’) — qûm in its decisive action sense. watᵉʾabbēd ʾet kol-zeraʿ hammamlāḵāh (‘she eliminated the entire royal family of Judah’) — ʾāḇad (H6) in the Piel: to destroy, put to death. zeraʿ hammamlāḵāh: the seed/offspring of the kingdom, i.e., all Davidic heirs. This is the Chronicler’s most dramatic moment of threat to the Davidic promise (cf. 21:7): only the nîr of 21:7 keeps the covenant alive through the hidden child Joash.
11: wayyiqqaḥ Yᵉhôšaḇʿat (‘Jehoshabeath took’) — her name Yᵉhôšaḇʿat means ‘the LORD swears’ or ‘the LORD is an oath-keeper’. The Chronicler adds unique information: she was ʾišet Yᵉhôyādāʿ hakkōhēn (wife of Jehoiada the priest) and ʾăḥôt ʾAḥazyāhû (sister of Ahaziah), making her both royal princess and priest’s wife — her dual status enables access to both the palace (to steal Joash) and the temple (to hide him). gānaḇ (H1589): to steal, take secretly.
12: wayhî ʾittām bᵉbêt hāʾĕlōhîm mitḥabbēʾ šēš šānîm (‘he remained hidden with them in the house of God for six years’) — ḥāḇāʾ (H2244, Hithpael): to hide oneself; the hidden Davidic heir in the temple is the Chronicler’s most pregnant image of the tension between dynastic threat and divine preservation. Six years of concealment in YHWH’s house while Athaliah rules — the contrast between the false ruler in the palace and the legitimate king hidden in the sanctuary is deliberate.
